coredata.dc:description: The micro-extraction and analysis of drugs in aquatic and various environments, such as food, edible tissues, and biological fluids, has recently received a lot of researchers’ attention for maintaining sustainability. Because of carbon dots (CDs) relative safety and low poisonousness, we will focus our review on CDs applications and ignore metallic quantum dots. Our review aims to provide a thorough understanding of sample preparation techniques used for analyzing pharmaceuticals in complicated matrices. The advantages of CDs over traditional carbon-based nanomaterials will be detailed. In addition, the fundamental tenets of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), their applications, and challenges in the magnetic micro solid-phase extraction process will be illustrated. As of right now, no published study has compiled the use of either CD@MIPs or magnetic MIPs in the analysis of various drugs or the removal of their residues. Many studies from 2019 to the first half of 2024 are examined and recorded in this review, representing a unique and novel collection. It can serve as a manual for creating and refining cutting-edge polymer-based analytical techniques in the pharmaceutical field. From pharmaceutical and greenness points of view, the most important aspects of MIPs’ practical application are their capacity, recyclability, and selectivity. Consequently, these items are enumerated with great care. In this article, we focus on the multi-template MIPs (mt-MIPs) and the functions and merits of the inclusion of cross-linkers, functional monomers, and graphene oxide in their tailoring. However, the need for using one multi-template MIP for the simultaneous capture of many drugs is deeply demanded in alignment with green chemistry perspectives.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The micro-extraction and analysis of drugs in aquatic and various environments, such as food, edible tissues, and biological fluids, has recently received a lot of researchers’ attention for maintaining sustainability. Because of carbon dots (CDs) relative safety and low poisonousness, we will focus our review on CDs applications and ignore metallic quantum dots.

Our review aims to provide a thorough understanding of sample preparation techniques used for analyzing pharmaceuticals in complicated matrices. The advantages of CDs over traditional carbon-based nanomaterials will be detailed. In addition, the fundamental tenets of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), their applications, and challenges in the magnetic micro solid-phase extraction process will be illustrated.

As of right now, no published study has compiled the use of either CD@MIPs or magnetic MIPs in the analysis of various drugs or the removal of their residues. Many studies from 2019 to the first half of 2024 are examined and recorded in this review, representing a unique and novel collection. It can serve as a manual for creating and refining cutting-edge polymer-based analytical techniques in the pharmaceutical field. From pharmaceutical and greenness points of view, the most important aspects of MIPs’ practical application are their capacity, recyclability, and selectivity. Consequently, these items are enumerated with great care. In this article, we focus on the multi-template MIPs (mt-MIPs) and the functions and merits of the inclusion of cross-linkers, functional monomers, and graphene oxide in their tailoring. However, the need for using one multi-template MIP for the simultaneous capture of many drugs is deeply demanded in alignment with green chemistry perspectives.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, it is widely acknowledged that the widespread use of various substances in different aspects of our modern lives inevitably results in their release into the environment and their presence in soil, water, atmosphere, and other surroundings. It may seem to the public that the situation has significantly deteriorated in the latest years, as a variety of emerging pollutants have been found in the environment for the first time[1]. This perception is definitely incorrect, as numerous compounds that are now being discovered in water, soil, and other environments may have been present for many years [2]. However, they remained undetected until recent advancements in analytical methods that can accurately detect even trace amounts. Due to significant advancements in analytical techniques for trace assessment, it is no wonder that an ever-growing range of foreign substances can now be identified and accurately measured in samples from the environment[3]. In certain instances, it remains a matter of debate whether they function as pollutants with or without ecological implications. Regardless, a thorough assessment of the effects of xenobiotics on the environment necessitates the presence of trustworthy data. This creates a need for advanced analytical techniques that can serve as the foundation for comprehensive monitoring initiatives [4].

The existence of drugs and compounds used in personal care products (PCPs) in water environments has recently received a lot of attention. Aside from the apparent release into municipal sewage, it is recognized that a number of these compounds can, depending on their chemical composition, even make it through sewage treatment plants, the primary entry points for PCPs into surface water systems [4]. Another route of entry into soil and, finally, water is formed when certain PCPs exhibit a significant sorption propensity in sewage sludge, which is employed to some degree for agricultural reasons [4]. The current thinking is that concentrations in surface water that are typically in the low ng/L range do not pose a major risk to the quality of drinking water [5]. The long-term effects of persistently low PCP concentrations on ecosystems, however, remain unclear. The emergence of germs resistant to antibiotics has been a significant concern since the advent of antibiotics, prompting much study on the subject. It is mandatory to do an environmental fate and impact analysis if the projected environmental concentration in surface water exceeds 10 ng/L, according to the published protocol on the environmental hazard determination of pharmaceuticals for human usage via the European Medicines Agency. Based on this recommendation, it is evident that the quantities of pharmaceuticals in the water may be significant in terms of potential effects on ecosystems, thanks to the development of more advanced analytical instruments [4]. Thus, accurate analytical data are crucial for conducting a thorough risk assessment of the environment. A continuous stream of articles addressing the need for improved methods for trace analysis of PCPs is therefore warranted [6,7].

The exposure of drug residues to various external elements, such as ultraviolet (UV) light, which causes photo-degradation that produces many components depending on the drug's structure and the degree of light exposure, is another problem related to their existence. Even at very low concentrations, some of these ingredients may still be harmful [8]. However, the coexistence of pollutants and pharmaceutical impurities with the active pharmaceutical ingredients poses a serious issue since they call for the use of both a precise and repeatable extraction technique in addition to a sensitive analytical technique [9].

Precise measurement of the desired medication from a complicated biological sample is crucial for analyzing its components, but it continues to be a difficult task. Contaminants present in the sample matrix can hinder the flow of the sample, cause fouling, and influence the pH and salt levels. These contaminants can obstruct the sampling and extraction devices, react with or extract alongside the desired analytes due to their affinity to the matrix, and pollute the instrumentation. For instance, when interferences like proteins bind to the target component, they will conceal it, impacting the measured concentration's precision and sensitivity. Furthermore, the quantities of analytes are typically found at exceedingly low levels in biological, dietary, and environmental sample matrices [10].

Consequently, there has been a growing focus on the advancement of sample preparation methods, such as preconcentration and isolation, in recent decades [11]. Varying quantities of target analytes necessitate distinct approaches to sampling, sample preparation, and clean-up. Currently, sample preparation is still regarded as the limiting factor in the whole analytical process [12]. Despite significant advancements in analytical instrumentation, achieving the stringent regulations for very low limits of detection (LODs) in environmental, food, and bio-analysis is still challenging. The presence of matrix components often hinders the effectiveness of most analytical methods, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), which, despite its potency, may be affected by matrix interference, which can either amplify or suppress the signals of the target ions. In recent times, there have been new goals established for sample preparation, as presented in Fig. 1
. [11].

The use of suitable sample preparation approaches significantly enhances the advancement of complex matrix analysis, as it is recognized as a crucial stage that accounts for around 80 % of the total time required for quantitative analysis. Liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) and solid-phase extraction (SPE) are considered to be significant sample preparation technologies that have been extensively employed for complex analysis. Nevertheless, novel sample preparation techniques, such as liquid phase microextraction (LPME), microdialysis (MD), micro solid-phase extraction (µ-SPE), and microfluidic devices, have been developed to address the weaknesses of unsatisfactory sensitivity, time-consuming operation procedures, and high costs [12]. These techniques aim to enhance sensitivity, streamline the process, and overcome emerging challenges. Simultaneously, contemporary LLE and SPE techniques have been progressing towards enhanced convenience and environmental sustainability. Currently, techniques that possess characteristics such as high throughput, autonomous operation, on-site capability, and a non-invasive nature are preferred for analyzing complicated samples.

While the other microextraction methods are still in their early stages of development, µ-SPE has already found widespread usage in analytical labs. Their high level of maturity, however, means that their regular usage is anticipated in the years to come [13].

Nevertheless, it must be emphasized that the extraction procedure relies on the separation of target analytes from the sample and an acceptor phase, which might be stationary or liquid phase. Present chromatographic methods impede the ultimate determination of the target analyte because, regardless of the selected microextraction technique, the various acceptor phases used to extract it cover the polarity scale, leading to the extraction of the analyte alongside other matrix compounds. The inherent benefits of microextraction methods are diminished due to the development of complicated applications requiring multiple washing solvents, which is made essential by the lack of selectivity during the extraction process [14].

Novel materials are currently being created to showcase cutting-edge analytical methodologies for intricate scientific apparatus. Nanomaterials, specifically carbon dots (CDs), are commonly employed in several fields, such as biological, food, and ecological research. In several fields of analytical chemistry, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have also emerged as valuable materials in recent years. MIPs are a kind of synthetic polymer that has been cross-linked by the copolymerization of a monomer and a cross-linker with the help of a template molecule [15].

MIPs have been used efficiently for chiral separation and assessment in the fields of pharmaceutical and forensic analytical chemistry [16–19]. Besides, MIPs are an effective tool for micro-chemical extractions in food, agriculture, and neuroscience [20–22].

Furthermore, there has been a lot of interest in the combination of MIPs and magnetic nanoparticles. Comparing the resultant magnetic MIPs to other media used for separation and adsorption, they show a number of encouraging properties. To begin with, magnetic nanoparticles are easily functionalized, easily manipulated, and have high surface areas and good biocompatibility [23].

Both magnetic MIPs and CD@MIPs have demonstrated significant promise in the following areas: sample cleaning and enrichment; evaluation and analysis of illegal drugs and their metabolites in various complex matrices; drug identification and quantification for forensic investigations and drug abuse monitoring; quality control analysis assisting in the differentiation of real drugs from fake or adulterated products; evaluation of the degree of drug contamination in the environment and comprehension of the potential ecological impacts; and detection of drugs and pharmaceutical compounds as sensing and bio-sensing platforms [24].

As carbon dots are produced from carbon sources, like waste materials or biomass, they are thought to be more environmentally friendly than quantum dots, which frequently involve the use of heavy metals like lead or cadmium, which can release hazardous substances during synthesis and disposal. In contrast, magnetic MIPs can use alternative magnetic materials that are free of heavy metals. It is commonly accepted that carbon dots are biocompatible, which is beneficial for the environment and human health [25].

Green synthesis techniques can be employed to create the magnetic materials and carbon dots used in CD@MIPs and magnetic MIPs. These techniques frequently include less trash production, solvents that are safe for the environment, and energy-efficient procedures [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparisons with other review articles in the aspects of magnetic MIPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: After reviewing the literature, several articles and reviews were published concerning different aspects of MIPs. Yanixia Liu et al. [27] published a review article that discussed different aspects, such as the principle of action of the traditionally used MIPs, optimization and evaluation of their imprinting efficiency, and the uses of MIPs in the recognition of biomolecules, as well as challenges and future aspects regarding the utilization of multiple target MIPs. MIPs can be used as antibody mimics, a method that was discussed by Karesten Haupt et al. [28]. They also talked about the method of synthesis and the applications of MIPs as bio-imaging of cancer biomarkers, covering the period from 2015 to 2020. On the other hand, Ben Moussa et al. [29] are concerned with their applications as electrochemical sensors for cancer biomarkers, representing the most advanced methods for their fabrication, including the use of nanomaterials and transduction strategies, their advantages and disadvantages. Moreover, Ruizehen Tian et al. [30] concentrated in their review article on the use of MIPs as artificial enzymes and how they were prepared for this purpose. Applications of MIPs for the diagnosis of targeted therapy and different strategies applied for their construction were considered by Balcer et al.[31]. Sheling Wang et al [32] published a review article discussing the issue of multi-template MIPs, methods of their preparation, their uses and importance, along with the problems, challenges, and future trends of their applications.

Some articles were developed to discuss the integration of CDs and MIPs, and others discussed MMIPs. Kecrli et al. [33] talked about the application of CD@MIPs for the preparation of fluorescent biosensors and their use for detecting different pollutants. They focused on eco-friendly methods for their synthesis. Furthermore, Sobiech and her team reported [34] an article concerned with QD/CD@MIP preparation methods and the most recent developments in QD/CD@MIP bioanalysis, encompassing analyses of biological, environmental, and food samples. Additionally, they highlighted the limitations of QD/CD@MIPs and sketched the opportunities to enhance QD/CD@MIP's potential in forthcoming bioanalytical processes.

Beibei Jia et al. [23] and Saqib Farooq et al. [25] published review articles about magnetic MIPs and their uses in the identification and adsorption of pollutants, mycotoxins, veterinary medication residues, pesticide residues, and adulteration in food. A few lingering issues and potential future developments to further boost magnetic MIPs features are also covered.

The use of magnetic MIPs or CD@MIPs in pharmaceutical analysis was not covered in any of the aforementioned articles. Therefore, the purpose of this study is to serve as a resource for the most recent and sophisticated uses of CD@MIPs and magnetic MIPs in the analysis of various medicines and their residues in various matrices utilizing various analytical techniques. As a result, our work may expand the applications of CD@MIPs and magnetic MIPs in several analytical domains, including food safety and environmental quality control. This is the first review article on MIPs that uses cutting-edge, environmentally benign technologies, such as carbon dots or magnetic cores, and how they can be used to detect, quantify, or remove various medications. Additionally, we will primarily focus on the advancement of sample preparation methods and evaluate their advantages in quantitative analysis. In addition, future trends will emphasize novel methodologies while also addressing prospects such as downsizing, high specificity, and mobility. This review carefully examines the newest advancements of CDs of this field, considering the aforementioned factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots (CDs) are a highly promising candidate
ce:section.ce:para: New materials are designed in order to suggest innovative analytical techniques for complex instrumentation. Among these are nanomaterials known as quantum dots (QDs) or carbon dots (CDs), which are employed in food, environmental, and medical investigations. While those nanomaterials have a lot going for them, functionalization is necessary to make sure they are useful. Although QDs were first discovered in the 1980 s [35–37], their full potential as semiconductor nanocrystals with physical dimensions smaller than the exciton Bohr radius (between 2 and 10 nm) was not fully realized until the late 1990 s [38,39], when they were first used as sensors in biology.

An inadvertent procurement of the CDs occurred around the early 2000 s [40–42]. This type of nanomaterial is composed of carbon particles with a size of less than 20 nm. The shell of the CDs is covered with functionalized groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, ether, carbonyl, or polymeric conjugates [43,44].

The primary constraint on the applicability of QDs is their toxicity when produced from core heavy atoms or when using dangerous chemicals during the synthesis process. In order to address these issues, CDs were created as very promising alternatives to QDs nano crystals due to their low toxicity, excellent water dispersibility, high conductivity, and ability to be synthesized from natural substrates including food, leaves, and fruits [45]. Furthermore, CDs are eco-friendly [46], biocompatible [47], easily synthesized [48] and functionalized [49], have tunable fluorescence and quantum yield (QY) [50,51], and have superior optical properties (e.g., strong absorption, bright photoluminescence, excellent light stability, and low photo bleaching thresholds) [52,53].

Four types of CDs may be distinguished in this scenario, categorized according to their fundamental structure, surface characteristics, and material properties: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon and polymer dots (CPDs), also known as polymeric nanodots. CNDs are characterized by amorphous cores; GQDs are comprised of diminutive sheets of graphene; and CPDs are hybrid structures that combine carbon cores with diverse functional groupings and chain-like polymers adorning their surfaces. Conversely, it is noteworthy to mention that CQDs, CNDs, and GQDs exhibit a spherical morphology characterized by their intricate multi-layered crystalline composition, wherein their surfaces are adorned with various chemical moieties [54]. It is the core's job to give CDs their fluorescent qualities, while the shell regulates the optical characteristics. The inherent characteristics that warrant consideration in this context encompass the phenomena of aqueous solubility, biological adaptability, and the latent capacity for integration with diverse materials [55]. CDs can have structural defects, e.g., edge disorders and grain boundaries. Besides, CDs can have surface traps that may result in a reduction of the observed fluorescence or luminescence. Because naked CDs don't interact well with biological systems and analytes, and because there's a chance that CDs with low QY (less than 5 %), they have to be modified before use. Many factors can limit CD’s quantum yield, including surface passivation related to non-radiative surface defects; various non-radiative energy dissipation pathways such as intersystem crossing, internal conversion, or transfer of energy to solvent molecules; small particle sizes of CDs tend to have high surface areas, which can lead to more surface defects and non-radiative pathways; excitons (electron-hole pairs) trapping and quenching at defect sites instead of undergoing radiative recombination; and photo bleaching, which is the irreversible loss of light-emission properties after long exposure to light. These limitations can be minimized via many approaches, such as efficient surface engineering, optimization of the synthesis process, and developing CDs with improved photo stability to decrease photo bleaching [55,56]. The functionalization of CDs may be accomplished primarily through two methods: surface modification and heteroatom doping. The employment of suitable methods and precursors during the synthesis of CDs allows for the incorporation of heteroatoms, which in turn moderates QY, shifts emission spectra, charge transfer ratio, orbital structures, surface and local chemical characteristics, and charge transfer ratio. It is possible to utilize more than one heteroatom in a single CDs doping procedure, and both metals and nonmetals are acceptable. Ions, organic compounds, polymers, DNA, and proteins can all be used to modify CD surfaces through surface functionalization. In the end, the procedure enhances QY while also giving CDs distinctive traits like a recognition functional group or catalytic capability. In conclusion, the functionalization and surface modification of CDs became an important step in the synthesis process, with the goal of producing a nanomaterial with the capacity to function in biological systems or with adequate characterization for analytical applications [34,56].

CDs have become increasingly popular in separation science because they offer several advantages over traditional carbon-based nanomaterials [57] as shown in Fig. 2 . It is important to note that the widespread usage of CDs has raised concerns about safety, both for human health and the overall ecology [58]. CDs have been identified as highly auspicious contenders due to their outstanding properties [45–53], as illustrated in Fig. 3 . Multiple investigations have been conducted on the utilization of altered CDs for the purpose of sample preparation.

The coupling of CDs with casts of MIPs has attracted considerable interest in this specific field. CD@MIP utilizes both the distinctive optical characteristics exhibited by CDs and the remarkable specificity demonstrated by MIPs. The capacity of MIPs to selectively attach to certain molecules is then transformed into an optical signal response by the CDs. The phenomenon of adsorption occurring on the MIPs frequently results in a proportional decrease in the intensity of the fluorescence of CDs. The attribute above bestows upon the substance a propensity for meticulous quantitative examination, making it a favorable option for fabricating sensors that exhibit heightened resilience, acuity, and discernment [59,60].

To preserve carbon dots' (CDs') optical qualities, structural integrity, and ability to resist aggregation, their cores must be stabilized. There are many methods for stabilizing CD cores, such as surface passivation, surface functionalization, polymer coating, inorganic encapsulation, cross-linking, and pH adjustment [34,56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fundamental tenets of the imprinting process and molecularly imprinted polymers
ce:section.ce:para: In the early 1970 s, a covalent method was proposed that uses reversible chemical interactions to create pre-organized structures prior to polymerization. This method created the concept of molecular imprinting [61]. Nevertheless, in the early 1980 s [62], a non-covalent approach drew interest from the analytical and medicinal sectors because of the ease with which host–guest complexes could be formed before polymerization, utilizing weak intermolecular interactions.

A MIP is defined by recognition zones that are three-dimensional and complementary to the template or structural counterparts in terms of molecular volume and functional group geometry [63]. Simultaneously, a control non-imprinted polymer is synthesized using an identical procedure to the synthesis of MIP, with the exception that the template is deliberately excluded. The MIPs demonstrate favorable specificity for the desired molecules (either the template or its structural equivalent) and offer several advantages over biological alternatives like antibodies or enzymes, as presented in Fig. 4 . The advantageous characteristics of MIPs are accountable for their utilization in several fields, including analytical chemistry, sensing, prospective medicine delivery, and chemical synthesis [64–66].

To do molecular imprinting, one must first acquire polymers that are structurally and binding site-specifically identical to template molecules. A functional monomer and a template molecule interact via the creation of covalent or non-covalent interactions in the fundamental synthesis of a MIP. Following the formation of many contact sites, the crosslinking agent is added. When the template molecule is taken out, imprinted holes that match the template molecule's spatial arrangement are created. This results in the formation of many action sites [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro solid-phase extraction application using molecularly imprinted polymers
ce:section.ce:para: Out of the several sample preparation procedures now available, µ-SPE meets almost all of the conditions indicated above [68]. The technique known as µ-SPE was first developed by Pawliszyn and Arthur [69] in the early 1990 s. It involves the separation of desired analytes by partitioning them between the stationary material and the analyzed sample. This stationary phase is usually coated onto the surface of a fused silica fiber. Traditionally, analytes were heated and released onto the injection site of a gas chromatograph, which is still a common practice today. There are other methods for their extraction, such as employing the mobile phase on an µ-SPE-HPLC interface that has been specifically constructed or extracting them in a small volume of a proper solvent for further chromatographic analysis[70]. Due to its ease of usage, compatibility with commercial fibers, lack of solvents, and potential for full automation, µ-SPE has become a popular tool for exact applications. Its use in chemistry labs is anticipated to continue developing in the near future [71].

One drawback of µ-SPE is that the sorbent is not very selective; this is especially problematic since commercially available fibers only span a certain range of polarities. The development of custom-made fibers to improve extraction selectivity has, thus, gained momentum in recent years. Some of the fibers coating processes that have been recently examined [72] include physical deposition, electrochemical approaches, and sol–gel technology. As a result of these processes, a variety of handmade coatings are available, which may mitigate some of the problems with commercial fibers. While some manufactured fibers have been used to determine target analytes in actual samples, most have not, making it impossible to demonstrate the anticipated enhancement of selectivity [72].

The analytical community often uses µ-SPE to study organic molecules because of the method's adaptability and ease of use. But nonselective sorbents form the basis of the currently available commercial fibers. A MIP is a durable polymer that can selectively recognize molecules thanks to the template that was used during its manufacture [73]. Various methods for obtaining MIP fibers for µ-SPE have been detailed in this study during the last few years [74].

Molecularly imprinted solid phase extraction, also known as MISPE, relies on MIPs as sorbents due to their intrinsic selectivity [73–76]. With the best features of both technologies—simplicity, flexibility, and selectivity—a potent analytical tool would be created by combining molecular imprinting with µ-SPE. Mullet et al. [77] first suggested a method to combine the two technologies in 2001; it included selectively determining propranolol in blood samples by putting MIP particles into a capillary for in-tube µ-SPE.

Using the devised approach, the benefits of in-tube µ-SPE—such as rapid operation, simplicity of automation, and high enrichment factors afforded by several draw/eject cycles—were readily apparent. Problems with this approach include the need for additional equipment (pump, multiport valves) and the fact that the mobile phase and solvent used to desorb analytes from the MIP are incompatible. This is a common issue with online MISPE protocols. So, the optimal solution would be to prepare silica fibers coated with a MIP and then use them to conduct µ-SPE. Koster et al. [78] provided a compelling case for this method in 2001 when they reported successfully synthesizing the first-ever silica fiber coated with MIP for µ-SPE of brombuterol from urine. A number of articles in the past few years, however, have revived interest in combining molecular imprinting with µ-SPE. The future scenarios of SPE involve advancements in miniaturization, automation, selective and efficient sorbent materials, hyphenation with analytical techniques, on-site portable and easily operated sorbents, and sustainable approaches. The use of more green or bio-based solvents and the development of reusable, sustainable, or recyclable sorbents are very attractive approaches for chemists. These approaches aim to improve the overall environmental impact of the extraction process. These advances will lead to expanding its range of applications, including pharmaceutical analysis [75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The utilization of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) for magnetic micro solid-phase extraction (µ-SPE)
ce:section.ce:para: The use of MNPs is becoming increasingly important in the ongoing study conducted by scientists and engineers, as it contributes significantly to various breakthroughs and treatments [79]. MNPs integrate inherent magnetic phenomena, such as superparamagnetism, with distinct nanoscale characteristics, such as enhanced reactivity due to significant surface-to-volume ratios [80]. Furthermore, MNPs have the ability to efficiently adsorb pollutants and may also be easily separated from the surrounding liquid using magnetic means, allowing for further treatment and elimination of the contaminants [81]. The coating and functionalization of MNPs can create a core–shell structure that allows for exceptional selectivity and sensitivity in targeting certain chemical and metallic contaminants [82]. MNPs have recently become prominent as materials used for labeling in the creation and construction of biosensors. This is done to enhance the sensitivity and stability of the sensors in order to identify environmental analytes more effectively. MNPs are incorporated into transducer materials or distributed throughout samples and are subsequently drawn towards the active detection surface of the biosensor using an external magnetic field. These MNP-based biosensors have various uses in the medical, food, and environmental sectors [83,84]. There has been a significant increase in interest in environmental chemo sensors that utilize MNPs that have been modified with chromogenic or fluorogenic substances. These molecules have the ability to specifically gather a particular pollutant. Additionally, when they capture the pollutant, they undergo a physicochemical transformation, which produces a detectable signal. This signal allows for rapid on-site detection [85]. Metallic nanoparticles, namely nanoscale zero-valent iron (nZVI), magnetite (Fe3O4), and maghemite (γ-Fe2O3) nanoparticles, are widely utilized and have generated significant attention in the field of environmental analysis. The inherent reactivity of iron, namely in the form of nZVI, can be harnessed to remediate soil and groundwater contamination by transforming contaminants into less harmful compounds. Consequently, MNPs are being employed in the environment for the purposes of preconcentration, detection, and elimination of pollutants [86]. Traditional techniques for producing MNPs necessitate the utilization of perilous compounds, the generation of noxious byproducts, and pollution from precursor components. Hence, there is an increasing demand to develop secure, non-hazardous, and ecologically sound methods for producing MNPs. Biological synthesis processes offer several significant advantages compared to widely employed methods. These include the employment of clean and ecologically friendly techniques, a reduction in the cost of the synthesis process, and great energy efficiency [87].

When employing various sorbents in SPE, achieving sufficient recovery of the analytes is not always feasible. Additionally, isolating and enriching particular compounds can be a labor-intensive and time-consuming process. Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) serve as a substitute for traditional solid-phase extraction (SPE) sorbents, effectively addressing the associated constraints, as shown in Fig. 5
[88]. In recent years, there has been a significant increase in the number of studies dedicated to the utilization of magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE). However, these publications focus on the use of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) that have been coated with a suitable inorganic or organic shell to isolate certain analytes of interest [89–92].

Preventing the oxidation of magnetite (Fe3O4) cores in magnetic MIPs is vital for maintaining their magnetic properties and stability [79,88]. There are many methods commonly employed to prevent the oxidation of magnetite in MIPs, such as: i) Coating the magnetite cores with a protective layer of inorganic coatings such as silica (SiO2), alumina (Al2O3), or titanium dioxide (TiO2). ii) Functionalizing the surface of magnetite cores with organic molecules with antioxidant properties, such as polyphenols or thiols. iii) Performing the synthesis and storage of magnetic MIPs under an inert atmosphere, such as nitrogen or argon. iv) Storing the magnetic MIPs in a dry and oxygen-free environment at low temperature, such as desiccators or airtight containers. v) The use of natural antioxidants in the MIP composite formulation, such as ascorbic acid (vitamin C) or tocopherols (vitamin E), can reduce the potential for oxidation [88].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecularly imprinted polymers with numerous templates for the simultaneous recognition of several targets
ce:section.ce:para: At the initial phase, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have the ability to identify only one specific target using a template molecule or ion. This constraint hinders their practical use in simultaneously identifying and eliminating several targets. Furthermore, doing preprocessing on each analyte separately in a sequential manner raises the likelihood of errors. Subsequently, the utilization of multiple-target templates facilitates the creation of multiple recognition sites inside a single polymeric material, known as multi-template molecularly imprinted polymers (mt-MIPs) [32]. Mt-MIPs refer to a type of polymer material that is tailored to have multiple templates imprinted within its structure. With the advent of the multi-template imprinting multiplex technique in the 2000 s, it became possible to use mt-MIPs to detect and capture many targets at once [93,94]. The use of mt-MIPs offers the advantage of reducing labor, time, and cost while also minimizing the need for chemical usage, making it an environmentally friendly approach in the field of green chemistry [95]. Despite its many benefits, it has some disadvantages as well, including higher cost, difficult template optimization, reduced binding affinity, complexity of synthesis, and bleeding (incomplete removal of template molecules from the polymer matrix, resulting in unwanted residues within the magnetic MIPs, lowering its selectivity). The likelihood of interactions between the many template molecules found in the polymer matrix presents another issue with mt-MIPs. This interaction may have a synergistic effect by providing a more diversified and complimentary binding environment, hence increasing the magnetic MIP recognition capability. Conversely, interactions may cause competition among the magnetic MIPs for binding sites if the binding locations or structural characteristics of the templates are similar, compromising the MMIPs' overall effectiveness by reducing their binding affinity and selectivity toward particular templates [95].

The capacity of mt-MIPs to simultaneously selectively detect both unrelated species, including inorganic and organic templates, and a variety of structurally comparable homologs or analogs is one of the sustainable development benefits of these platforms [96]. Mt-MIPs are widely used for target recognition, cleanup, removal, or distribution due to their exceptional selectivity, precision, and capacity for adsorption [97]. Nevertheless, the process of commercializing mt-MIPs remains sluggish. Currently, single-template MIPs are more prevalent in the market than multi-template MIPs (mt-MIPs), indicating that there are still obstacles to overcome in the commercialization of mt-MIPs. The research on mt-MIPs is now motivated by the requirement for monitoring and quantitatively detecting several analytes. Several highly favorable reviews have offered comprehensive insights into single-target molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), with a specific emphasis on advanced application investigations of MIPs in pretreatment [98], chromatographic [99], electrophoretic separations [100], and sensing [101]. These reviews also discuss the existing problems and potential future prospects [102]. As far as we know, only a limited number of evaluations highlight various advanced methodologies and prospective applications of mt-MIPs [103]. In this article, we focus on the current advancements in mt-MIPs, specifically the different techniques used for sample pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussions
ce:section.ce:para: The uncontrolled disposal of drugs and pharmaceutical formulations can lead to several environmental and health problems. Disposal of drugs into the housing water system can lead to contamination of water and soil. As a result, these chemical compounds can be permanent and may have undesired effects on aquatic ecosystems, including fish and other aquatic organisms[4]. Besides, they can also contaminate drinking water sources, posing potential risks to human health. The groups of pharmaceuticals are very diverse. Some drugs are rapidly metabolized and excreted in urine, while others have long pharmacokinetic parameters and stay in the body for a long time. The presence of pharmaceuticals in the environment can lead to the known phenomenon of drug resistance in bacteria and other microorganisms. Exposure to low levels of drugs over time can enhance the proliferation of resistant strains. Besides, pharmaceutical compounds can have toxic effects on many organisms, disturbing the ecological balance and actually impacting the overall health of ecosystems. Moreover, traces of drugs in water resources can enter the food chain, potentially affecting human consumers [104]. The unsafe disposal of pharmaceuticals can lead to the propagation of these compounds and their related impurities in a variety of matrices, such as water, soil, milk, and foods, affecting human health and the environmental ecosystem. Notably, drugs may be toxic for the ecosystem as intact drugs, degradation products, and related metabolites [9]. Therefore, it is important to encourage perfect drug disposal protocols, such as drug take-back programs, using specific collection sites, and following specific disposal guidelines. Besides, green drug formulations and disposal could improve their safety [105]. Therefore, there is a great need to develop efficient and sustainable µ-SPE techniques, including MIPs, to capture these drugs and their metabolites from different matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of magnetic MIPs for selective adsorption of certain drugs
ce:section.ce:para:
The investigated examples of magnetic MIPs for selective adsorption of certain drugs in the last few years are collectively listed in Table 1
.

There are several drugs that are metabolized in the human body and eliminated as different chemical entities, which can potentially impact the human environment. The antihypertensive drug propranolol is an example of these drugs. It undergoes extensive metabolism in the liver, resulting in many related metabolites. These metabolites, including 4-hydroxypropranolol and glucuronide conjugates, are eliminated in urine and feces. Surface waters have been found to contain propranolol and its metabolites [77]. Ciprofloxacin is another example. Ciprofloxacin is a broad-spectrum antibiotic used to treat bacterial infections. It is metabolized in the liver, and the primary metabolites, including desethylene ciprofloxacin and oxociprofloxacin, are eliminated via urine and feces. Surface waters and wastewater have been found to contain ciprofloxacin and its metabolites, which raises concerns regarding the potential development of antibiotic resistance in environmental microorganisms [126]. Naproxen is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) commonly used to reduce pain. It undergoes hepatic metabolism, resulting in the formation of several metabolites, e.g., 6-O-desmethylnaproxen. Both naproxen and its metabolites have been detected in wastewater treatment plant effluents and surface waters [148]. 17β-Estradiol is an estrogen hormone that controls human reproductive system. It is metabolized in the liver and excreted mainly in urine. The primary metabolites of 17β-estradiol include estrone and estriol. 17β-Estradiol and its metabolites have been detected in wastewater, surface waters, and even drinking water sources. 17β-Estradiol and its metabolites can lead to hormonal imbalances and reproductive diseases [153].

On the other side, there are several drugs that are eliminated from the body without noteworthy metabolism. Sulfamethoxazole and trimethoprim are often combined to produce a synergistic effect for the treatment of many bacterial infections. Similar to trimethoprim, sulfamethoxazole is eliminated through the kidneys without significant metabolism. It is excreted predominantly in the urine as an intact molecule [119]. Doxycycline is another example of an antibiotic that is eliminated mainly through renal excretion. Doxycycline undergoes minimal metabolism in the body, and the majority of this antibiotic is eliminated unchanged [158]. These antibiotics are examples of drugs that are eliminated from the body without undergoing significant metabolism in the urine or feces. Therefore, there is a need for proper management of pharmaceutical wastes to minimize environmental hazards.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples for the capture of antihistaminic and antidepressant tricyclic drugs from water environments
ce:section.ce:para: Many antihistaminic and antidepressant tricyclic compounds (TCC) such as amitriptyline, cyproheptadine, desloratadine, ketotifen fumarate, and 8-chloroazatadine are commonly used by humans and illegally used as feed supplements to encourage animals' growth and raise the rate of meat production. Numerous of these medications are not metabolized by the body and are expelled into the environment through feces or urine. The majorities of these medications, meanwhile, have a prolonged half-life in the environment and can find their way into the food and water systems. This may seriously threaten people's health and the safety of the local ecological environment by directly affecting the central nervous system and destroying neuroendocrine signals. Hence, the removal of the residue of these drugs from the environment is a big challenge for chemists for the sake of general public health and the protection of the environment [113].

The high surface areas of Fe3O4 nanoparticles enhanced the separation of MIPs significantly, reducing money, time, and labor. The specific magnetic MIPs for this class was reported by Cao and his team, where cyproheptadine was the template molecule, MAA was the functional monomer, 2,2′-azoisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was the initiator compound, and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) was the efficient cross-linking agent. The sorption capacities for the investigated TCC ranged from 42.14 to 48.89 mg/g, which are higher than the values for NIPs. It took 25 min for the sorption to occur. The selectivity was investigated in the presence of terbutaline and clonidine, where the sorbent selectivity towards TCC was achieved. This tailored magnetic MIP could be reused efficiently for up to ten cycles. Therefore, this sorbent was selective, efficient, and sustainable for TCC capture in water environment [113].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of estimated target antibiotics (norfloxacin)
ce:section.ce:para: The wide range of antimicrobial activity of norfloxacin (NOR), a third-generation quinolone drug, has made it valuable in veterinary medicine for treating animal infections [115]. However, because of the body's inadequate metabolism, NOR residues frequently remain in food and edible tissues. In Hong Kong, NOR was found in preschoolers' urine, chicken liver, and chicken muscles. Humans can experience severe negative consequences from excessive NOR residues in food, including sadness, nausea, and dizziness. As a result, NOR determination is highly significant for the security of food and human wellness. Besides, effective extraction is necessary before the instrumental analysis in order to determine the net organic residue (NOR) in complicated foods [115]. NOR residues are present in many samples, such as milk [115], different water environments [116,117], and pork liver [118]. Therefore, many extractive techniques have been tailored for the capture of NOR from food stuff, e.g., QuEChERS and solid phase extraction (SPE). However, the recorded adsorption capacity and the selectivity of these techniques remain inadequate [159]. As a result of these shortcomings, Cao and his Chinese fellows [115] reported a magnetic nanoparticle MIP for the capture of norfloxacin from milk.

The functional monomer was dopamine. The magnetic MIP was formulated via the self-polymerization of dopamine molecules on the surface of APTES-reformed Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The sorption capacity was 14.2 mg/g. It took 60 min for the sorption to occur. The selectivity of the method was proved in the coexistence of other five antibiotics, e.g., tetracycline hydrochloride, sulfadiazine, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, and danofloxacin. The magnetic MIP could be reused efficiently for up to six cycles without a great reduction in NOR recoveries. Therefore, this sorbent is sensitive, selective, efficient, and sustainable if compared to the aforementioned traditional extraction methods for NOR capture from food matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of estimated target antibiotics (tetracyclines)
ce:section.ce:para: Other examples of commonly used antibiotics to treat and prevent infections in animals are tetracyclines (TCs). The four TCs studied are tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DC), and chlorotetracycline (CTC). Because of their excellent qualities and inexpensive cost, TCs are frequently used as additives to feed veterinary medications and farm animals. However, TCs can contaminate water through the excretion of waste from animals containing TCs and the waste water discharged from animal farms and pharmaceutical industries. Numerous health risks for humans, including antibiotic resistance, allergies, and digestive problems, can be brought on by the excessive accumulation of TCs in human water and foods. Therefore, TCs assay and removal are greatly important for the safety of human health. Besides, effective extraction is necessary before the real analysis in order to determine the net organic residue (TCs) in complex water matrixes [160]. A few techniques for removing TC from water include photodegradation and electrochemistry. However, because of inadequate light source conditions and high energy requirements, their efficiencies are very limited. Using a variety of sorbents, such as magnetic carbon composites, biochar, magnetic resin, rice husk ash, wasted coffee grounds, and other materials, the adsorption technique is a quick and effective way to capture TC. The practical applications of these adsorbents are limited due to their low capacities, long equilibrium times, and unsatisfactory selectivity [160].

In this context, molecularly imprinted techniques can present an ideal solution for the selective identification of target antibiotic in complicated matrices. These three-dimensional active sites become very selective sorbent materials for TCs because they match the target antibiotics in shape, size, and functional groups. Solid supports with relatively large superficial areas are needed to enhance the adsorption power of MIPs. The oxygen-rich GO is regarded as an ideal carrier for the synthesis of MIP and for improving the kinetic rates and rebinding capability [161].

The multi-template MIPs tailored for TCs were made of modified silica magnetic graphene oxide (MS@MGO), and TEOS was the cross-linker. The sorption capacity for the novel MS@MGO@MIP was 40.09 mg/g. TCs had an adsorption period of as long as 60 min. Selectivity was assured via the estimation of binding affinity for the novel MS@MGO@MIP in the coexistence of two structural analogs, namely ciprofloxacin hydrochloride and methacycline. The maximum loss in the adsorption recoveries for TCs via the reported MS@MGO@MIP was only 10 percent, even after five successive adsorption and desorption cycles [160].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Function and types of cross-linkers and functional monomers that are commonly used in pharmaceutical applications of MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Three steps contribute to the MIP synthesis technique. Initially, covalent or non-covalent interactions between the template and functional monomer molecules achieve the pre-polymerization complex. The cross-linker exists mainly once the polymerization process happens in the second phase. Finally, the cross-linked polymer matrix is cleared out of the template [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Function of cross-linkers and the most commonly used types
ce:section.ce:para: The function of cross-linkers in MIPs is to generate a three-dimensional network structure within the polymer matrix that can improve the MIPs binding affinity for the target analytes based on their size, shape, and functional groups. Furthermore, selectivity increases markedly if the cross-linker makes up more than 50 % of the mixture used for pre-polymerization [162]. The most frequently used cross-linkers in MIP fabrications used for drug analysis are Ethylene Glycol Dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and Tetraethyl ortho silicate (TEOS), while the most abundant cross-linkers in MIPs@CDs used for drug analysis are (TEOS) and (EGDMA).

EGDMA has many advantages as a cross-linker in MIPs, including high cross-linking efficiency. EGDMA has two methacrylate groups that can go through radical polymerization reactions, allowing for the establishment of strong cross-links. Besides, EGDMA allows controlling the final mechanical properties of MIPs via adjustment of cross-linker concentration and polymer density, as well as magnetic particle encapsulation. Furthermore, EGDMA-based cross-linked MIPs exhibit good chemical resistance and optimal thermal stability. Therefore, they can be used efficiently in high-temperature situations [163].

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) is the second most frequently used cross-linker in MIPs and the first one in CDs. The TEOS cross-linker has many merits, including cross-linking efficiency, as it can form a stable three-dimensional polymer matrix. TEOS has many other characteristics, such as enhanced stability for the polymer matrix, compatibility with several materials, low cost-effectiveness, and controllable porosity. The polymer porosity is very crucial in controlling pore size distribution, surface area, and the availability of active binding sites [164].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Function of functional monomers and the most commonly used types
ce:section.ce:para: In contrast, the role of functional monomers in MIPs is to facilitate the binding and recognition of target molecules. Besides, they are essential compounds in the tailoring of active binding sites and the imprinting process in general [34]. The most frequently used functional monomers in MIPs tailoring are methacrylic acid (MAA) and 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES), while the most abundant functional monomer in MIPs@CDs is (APTES).

MAA functional monomer has many merits in the tailoring of MIPs. These advantages could be shortened as follows: i) It can form a strong hydrogen bond with a variety of investigated compounds because of the carboxylic acid group; ii) MAA can be flexibly copolymerized with a variety of other hydrophilic and/or hydrophobic monomers and cross-linkers; iii) The ionization of MAA is pH-dependent, therefore it is suitable for selective recognition and release of the target analyte; iv) MAA displays convenient stability and compatibility with many solvents; v) MAA is a commercially available monomer vi) MAA is a versatile monomer with a wide range of applications in the recognition of target compounds [165,166].

The advantages of the alkylamine APTES as a functional monomer in MIPs can be stated as follows: i) APTES has many functional groups, such as the active amino group that can form covalent and hydrogen bonds; ii) APTES can form stable and durable covalent bonds with the polymer matrix; iii) enhanced binding affinity and selectivity related to the amino group; iv) APTES can be attached to the surface of MIPs, enhancing surface functionality and selectivity via the introduction of new functional groups; v) APTES is compatible with many polymerization techniques, e.g., surface imprinting and precipitation polymerization; iv) APTES monomer has been efficiently applied in many analytical fields such as pharmaceutical and environmental types[167].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The challenges of the construction of composite magnetic MIPs and CD@MIPs and the challenges of functionalization of the core
ce:section.ce:para: From a chemical point of view, the construction of composite magnetic MIPs and CD@MIPs can have many challenges, including i) the compatibility of composite components: The synthesis processes of these sorbents require the mixing of many components, such as the polymer matrix, functional monomers, cross-linkers, and template molecules. Therefore, the compatibility issue between these different components should be carefully controlled to form stable composite structures. ii) Optimization of various synthesis chemical reactions such as polymerization, functionalization, and cross-linking. iii) The complete and efficient removal of templates while maintaining the structural integrity of the imprinted is a very challenging step and requires careful optimization of washing liquids and extraction procedures. iv) Optimization of composite performance in terms of desired binding affinity, selectivity, and reusability. v) The presence of interfering analogues in the sample matrix can affect the performance of these composites [34,62,63].

The functionalization of the cores of magnetic MIPs and CD@MIPs can have certain limits, such as i) surface accessibility, i.e., the availability of surface sites for the attachment of functional groups. ii) Stability and compatibility of the magnetic cores with the functional groups being attached. Magnetic cores are often composed of materials like iron oxide or other magnetic nanoparticles, and their surface properties need to be carefully considered to prevent degradation or loss of magnetic properties during functionalization. Additionally, the functional groups should be compatible with the core material to avoid any adverse reactions or changes in the physical properties of the MIP. iii) Binding site accessibility: The functionalization of the magnetic MIP cores should not hinder the accessibility of the imprinted binding sites. iv) The density and distribution of the functional groups on the magnetic MIP cores can influence the binding capacity and selectivity. The functional groups should be sufficiently dense and homogeneously distributed on the surface to ensure optimal specificity. v) Limits in synthesis methodology and reaction conditions [34,62,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-template MIPs pharmaceutical applications and merits
ce:section.ce:para:
Magnetic MIPs are usually formulated to fit a specific drug, as illustrated in Table 1. As a result, MIPs with a single template cannot effectively and simultaneously remove several components. Dual-template and multi-template MIPs have lately appeared as solutions to this challenge, and chemists are paying great interest to them. Multi-template MIPs outperform single-template MIPs in specific molecular recognition [168]. Furthermore, in a few cases, MIPs have been designed to fit many drugs of related chemical structures, as demonstrated in Table 2
, e.g., cephalosporins [169], such as cephalexin, cefoperazonetrace, and cefazolin; quinolones [170], such as fleroxacin, enoxacin, norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, and lomefloxacin hydrochloride; carbamazepine and oxcarbazepine [171]; tetracycline [160], doxycycline, tetracycline, chlortetracycline, and oxytetracycline.

An innovative magnetic nano graphene oxide MIP was reported by Nurrokhimah and his researchers [169] for the simultaneous extraction of three cephalosporins, namely, cephalexin, cefoperazonetrace, and cefazolin, from milk samples prior to the high-performance liquid chromatography-diode array detector (HPLC-DAD) assay. Selectivity is enhanced by the MIP, and extraction affinity is improved by the use of graphene oxide. The capacities for adsorption were 2.6, 2.9, and 2.8 µg/g, referring to high binding for the aforementioned MIP with cephalosporins. The specificity was checked in the presence of two analogs, e.g., ceftazidime and ceftriaxone, where high affinities for the three cephalosporins were observed. Notably, up to five reuses of the MIP adsorbent are possible without noticeably reducing extraction effectiveness. The method had the merit of extracting the three cephalosporins simultaneously. Therefore, this nano graphene oxide MIP is considered the first reported multi-template one.

Multi-template MIPs were also introduced by Fan and his team in 2019 for the pre-concentration of trace concentrations of quinolones in real aqueous samples. The novel multi-pattern magnetic graphene oxide MIPs showed outstanding performance in terms of sensitivity and applicability, where the LOD was 11 ng.L-1 in the Norfloxacin example. Generally, in all cases of multi-pattern MIPs, recoveries were very convenient, ranging from 89.67 to 100.50 %. Besides, adsorption capacities were promising too, ranging from 9.51 to 40.09 mg/g. Quinolones had an adsorption time of less than 12 min [170].

Graphene oxide (GO) MIP was used in the synthesis of all the aforementioned multi-template MIPs except for the carbamazepine one. GO can serve multiple functions in multi-template MIPs [32,173]. Generally, the insertion of GO in multi-template MIPs can enhance binding selectivity, capacity, stability, and enable additional functionalities, making them favorable adsorbents for various pharmaceuticals’ separation and sensing. Particularly, GO can enhance the multi-template MIP properties as follows: i) Enhancing selectivity of the magnetic MIPs; ii) improving template binding as GO has a large surface area and can form strong interactions with many chemical groups iii) GO has excellent mechanical and thermal stability. Therefore, its presence improves the reusability of MIPs; iv) GO is a highly conductive substance, therefore its inclusion in the MIPs can improve the electrical conductivity of the polymer matrix; v) dispersion and stabilization during the polymerization procedure. Thus, its inclusion improves MIP’s reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examples of magnetic CD@MIPs for selective adsorption of certain drugs
ce:section.ce:para: Referring to the wide propagation of TCs in veterinary medicines and feed as previously reported in subsection [7.1 Examples of estimated target antibiotics: tetracycline], Removal and elimination of TCs from various environments received great attention [6 out of 12 drugs listed in Table 3 ] from analysts using magnetic CD@MIPs, as illustrated in Table 3.

In this context, a unique Mg/N co-doped CD was revealed by Hu and his Chinese team [174] in conjunction with MIP for the detection and quantitation of tetracycline in animal feeds. The use of Mg/N-CDs as a signal tag can greatly increase the fluorescence sensor's sensitivity. The high-blue fluorescent CD was measured at 455 nm as an emission wavelength, while the excitation wavelength was 350 nm. Methacrylic acid (MAA) was used as an active functional monomer, while ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) was used as an efficient cross-linker. Tetracycline was the template molecule. The LOD was 0.79 ng mL− 1. Up to six reuses of the aforementioned sensor are possible without noticeable loss in recovery. The drugs oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, chloramphenicol, and enrofloxacin were tested in the selectivity test, and the quenching effect for tetracycline was the highest, as documented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Section highlights
ce:section.ce:para: The function of cross-linkers in MIPs is to generate a three-dimensional network structure within the polymer matrix that can improve the MIPs binding affinity for the target analytes. The most frequently used cross-linkers in MIPs and CD@MIPs fabrications used for drug analysis are Ethylene Glycol Dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and Tetraethyl Orthosilicate (TEOS). In contrast, the role of functional monomers in MIPs is to facilitate the binding and recognition of target molecules. The most frequently used functional monomers in MIPs and CD@MPIs tailoring are methacrylic acid (MAA) and 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES).

There are several drugs that are metabolized in the human body and eliminated as different chemical entities, which can potentially impact the human environment, such as the antihypertensive drug propranolol and ciprofloxacin. On the other side, there are several drugs that are eliminated from the body without noteworthy metabolism, such as sulfamethoxazole, trimethoprim, and doxycycline.

Notably, magnetic MIPs are usually formulated to fit a specific drug. However, in a few cases, they have been designed to fit many drugs of related chemical structures, e.g., cephalosporins, quinolones, carbamazepine, and tetracycline classes. Graphene oxide (GO) MIP was used in the synthesis of all the aforementioned multi-template MIPs except for the carbamazepine one. GO can serve multiple functions in multi-template MIPs. Particularly, the insertion of GO in multi-template MIPs can enhance the binding selectivity, capacity, stability, and enable additional functionalities, making them favorable adsorbents for various pharmaceuticals’ separation and sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The challenges of the applications of MIPs and CDs in the field of drug analysis
The challenges of the applications of MIPs in drug analysis
ce:section.ce:para: While MIPs have several merits, there are also challenges and shortcomings associated with their use in drug analysis. These challenges can be shortened as follows: i) selection for the appropriate template molecule, particularly for the complex drug molecule with stereochemistry variants or numerous functional groups; ii) achieving high imprinting efficiency and specificity is very crucial i.e., targeting specific drug molecules in complicated matrices with structurally similar compounds; iii) controlling the polymerization conditions and optimizing the synthesis procedure can be challenging, leading to variations between batches and partial reproducibility of MIPs; iv) The kinetics of binding between MIPs and target drugs can be slower compared to natural body receptors or antibodies that may affect the time of the analysis; v) MIPs may have a limited capacity, which can result in poor accuracy when analyzing drugs of low concentration levels in complex biological matrices; vi) sample matrix interference, particularly in biological fluids or complex environmental samples; vii) cost-effectiveness and scalability: the fabrication of MIPs on a large scale is usually a costly and non-easy process [187,188]. However, continuous research is going on, particularly in the synthesis of MIPs from natural non-hazard sources to support their greenness, cost-effectiveness, recyclability, and productivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The challenges of the applications of CDs in drug analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The main shortcomings could be listed in the following items: i) their standardization and characterization: CDs can be synthesized via several methods, resulting in a wide range of physical and optical properties. Therefore, standardization of their synthesis protocols is very important to guarantee their reproducibility. ii) CDs tend to aggregate in variable conditions, e.g., pH and temperature that lead to their instability and unreliability of results. Therefore, perfect storage of CDs is essential to maintain their stability; iii) ensuring the selectivity of CDs towards the target drug or metabolite while reducing the matrix effect in complex biological matrices is a challenging task; iv) attaining a low limit of detection for low drug concentrations in complicated biological matrices is a hard task because of background signals and/or low fluorescence quantum yield; v) although their relative safety if compared to quantum dots, the in vivo applications of CDs necessitate careful assessment for their safety and biocompatibility; vi) cost-effectiveness and scalability: the production of high-grade CDs on a large scale is usually a costly and time-consuming process [189–191].
More up-to-date research is strongly required in the fields of CDs green synthesis and optimization, surface functionalization techniques, and reduction of background signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the application of MIPs and/or CD@MIPs in an analytical procedure from a greenness point of view
ce:section.ce:para: From a greenness point of view, several factors should be considered regarding the application of the final product, whether it is a sorbent (MIPs) or a sensor (CD-MIPs), in an analytical procedure. i) Green chemistry codes, such as minimizing waste generation, using renewable natural resources, minimizing energy consumption, minimizing the environmental footprint, and reducing hazardous materials, should be followed during the production of sorbents and sensors [25,26]. ii) Low energy consumption during the analytical procedure can reduce the overall carbon footprint and assure analytical methods greenness. iii) Minimizing the use of hazardous chemicals, reducing waste generation, and implementing recycling strategies enrich the green aspects. Besides, the use of greener solvents such as ethanol and avoiding toxic reagents such as acetonitrile is also an essential factor [87]. iv) Reducing the volume of analyzed samples and implementing miniaturized analytical platforms. v) Reusability and recyclability, i.e., the use of MIPs or CD@MIPs that can be reused several times without losing their efficiencies, can reduce the need for continuous production and disposal of new materials. vi) Conducting a life cycle assessment (LCA) of the final sorbents and sensors and their applications can provide a comprehensive point of view for their environmental impact, including raw material extraction, production, use, and disposal [192,193]. It helps identify weaknesses, strengths, and potential areas for improvement towards more sustainable procedures [163]. The total environmental risks of the analytical process can be reduced by taking into account all of these variables and implementing greener practices throughout the manufacture and use of the final sorbents and sensors [194,195]. This is in line with the concepts of sustainable development and green chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions and future perspectives
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular imprinting technique has proven to be an effective tool in the development of highly selective analytical processes in the field of pharmaceutical analysis. But adding it to micro solid-phase extraction (µ-SPE) hadn't been thought of before. Molecular imprinting and µ-SPE can work together, according to studies released in the past few years on the subject, which bodes well for future studies in this field.

Various approaches have been made possible in the production of imprinted fibers. Each method has its own set of pros and cons, and thus far, any one of them has shown to be the best. Analytes from a broad variety of environmental, food, and bio samples have been effectively extracted using the many imprinted fibers that have been developed. The resulting fibers' porosity and, by extension, the ease with which target analytes may attach to certain binding sites are two areas that obviously need improvement. Therefore, at this point in time, it is essential to do comprehensive investigations on the effects of various cross-linkers and porogens. Conversely, fresh approaches to polymerization that make it simpler to regulate the fiber thickness are anticipated to emerge in the near future.

Scholars are drawn to the issue of antibiotic resistance and the spread of antibiotics in the environment. Many antibiotics, such as TCs and NOR, are frequently used as additives to feed veterinary medications and farm animals. These antibiotics can contaminate water through the excretion of waste from animals containing antibiotics and the waste water discharged from animal farms and pharmaceutical industries. Numerous health risks for humans, including antibiotic resistance, allergies, and digestive problems, can be brought on by the excessive accumulation of antibiotics in human water and foods. Therefore, antibiotic analysis and removal are greatly important for the safety of human health. The antibacterial class accounted for the majority of the recorded MIPs pharmaceutical publications and the reported CD@MIPs pharmaceutical articles.

Generally, magnetic MIPs show greater values for sorption capacities if compared to magnetic non-imprinted polymers (MNIPs). Selectivity tests were done in most of the investigated articles by comparing the affinities of MIPs toward the target medicine to those of analogues of related structures and non-analogues of dissimilar structures, whereas the binding affinity for the target drug is usually higher. Reusability is the most important factor in terms of sustainability. In most of the studied MIP sorbents, recyclability was confirmed for many sequential cycles. However, in a few examples, regeneration and selectivity assessments were missed. Therefore, it is extremely recommended in the application of MIP protocols to consider selectivity and recyclability tests as mandatory items before applications. The limitations of poor yield of CDs can be minimized via many approaches, such as efficient surface engineering, optimization of the synthesis process, and developing CDs with improved photo stability to decrease photo bleaching.

In conclusion, we believe that molecular imprinting combined with µ-SPE has the potential to become an efficient tool that analytical labs frequently utilize in the near future, even if it is still in its early stages. The future scenarios of SPE involve advancements in miniaturization, automation, selective and efficient sorbent materials, hyphenation with different analytical techniques, on-site portable sorbents, and sustainable approaches for the reduction of consumed solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future trends regarding MMIPs and CD@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Enhancing the sensitivity and selectivity of MMIPs and CD@MIPs is imperative for enhancing their performance in applications such as drug delivery, biomolecule analysis, and environmental monitoring. Besides, multifunctional sensing or separation platforms may be made possible by the integration of MMIPs with other functional materials, such as fluorescence probes or catalytic nanoparticles. This may create new opportunities for their applications in new fields such as drug discovery and diagnostics. In-depth studies on MMIPs and CD@MIPs, which are employed in bio-imaging, drug targeting, controlled release systems, and specific identification of various disease markers or specific cells, enable personalized treatment and improve therapeutic outcomes. Additionally, it is extremely recommended in the application of MIP protocols to consider the selectivity and recyclability tests as mandatory items before applications. Using multi-targeting MIPs to extract constituents from various chemical classes whose structures are chemically unrelated is also advisable. More up-to-date research is strongly required in the fields of CD@MIPs green synthesis and optimization, surface functionalization techniques, and reduction of background signals. Additional efforts should be directed towards the synthesis of MIPs from natural, non-hazardous sources to support their greenness, cost-effectiveness, recyclability, and productivity. Looking for environmentally friendly solvents other than methanol and acetonitrile, which are the most widely utilized, is deeply required from a green point of view. Finally, assessments of greenness pictograms for the reported pharmaceutical applications using AGREEprep or complexGAPI automated platforms will help identify weaknesses, strengths, and potential areas for improvement towards more sustainable procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the writing of this analytical review, the authors used QuillBot.com in order to improve article readability and check for grammar mistakes. After using this tool or service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and took full responsibility for the content of the publication.
